result:
The world is both wider and smaller than we ever imagine. Even as I sit here composing these words, some part of me wonders who you are, and what you’re hoping to find—if anything—in these next thousand words. The act of writing is always, in some ways, a guess; like tossing a message in a bottle into the current and hoping someone will find it, maybe months or even years from now. Maybe that’s too poetic a start, but if you’ve granted me this space, I feel compelled to honor the uncharted possibility of it.

What comes to mind are mornings in late spring—early golden sunlight crawling across the floor, the smallest, nearly invisible particles of dust dancing in the glow. The day is neither cool nor warm; change hangs in the air. If you listen carefully, you can hear the world stretching, waking, the birds negotiating their territories, the trees shaking their new leaves in the slight breeze. Even in the midst of urban sprawl, life abounds at every scale. I am struck by this tininess, this grandeur, the unfurling of every moment.

Yet, at the same time, life is deeply ordinary. It is full of grocery lists, urgent emails, car horns, and the mysterious rattling of a pipe somewhere in the walls. We are shaped by routines—the comfort of a familiar route to work, the ritual of coffee brewed just so. Sometimes, drifting through these daily tasks, I wonder if I am missing the true heart of it all, and if that is simply part of being human.

But then, unexpected beauty catches my eye: the way a stranger smiles at a child, a sudden sunshower sparkling on asphalt, the way music can transform the air around us in an instant. There is a poem by Lisel Mueller that says, “Nothing is louder than the end of a song, the day the music stopped.” And I think of how we return, again and again, to quiet, to wonder, hoping to find something meaningful even as the world whirls on.

Have you ever noticed how nostalgia is both sweet and painful, a tug that draws us back and reminds us, relentlessly, of the passage of time? I think of high school notebooks, old letters folded in shoeboxes, the mismatched memories of friendships that faded. Technology puts all of history and humor at our fingertips, and yet sometimes the simplest memory—a late August sunset, the sound of cicadas—can move us in ways we can’t quite explain.

Maybe that is the essence of living: noticing. Paying attention. Holding on to the delight hidden in ordinary things, the brief revelations that sparkle in the everyday. There is a kind of grace in looking more closely, peering into forgotten corners, seeing that everything changes but everything endures in some form. 

Consider how language, of all things, outlives its speakers—the carved names on a crumbling stone, a child repeating the same rhyme her grandmother once sung. Words are little vessels of time and memory, carrying our stories forward. Perhaps that’s why it feels so important to write, to speak, to read aloud: we hope, somehow, to leave a trace.

Of course, meaning is slippery. Some days, the world feels ordered and bright; on others, it is messy, random, even cruel. If there is a point to be made from these wandering thoughts, it is perhaps this: we are all making our way in the dark, fumbling for a light switch, and somehow we manage to collide in moments of genuine connection. That’s what art is for, I think—not to make sense but to forge a link, however brief, between strangers who might never meet.

I wonder what would happen if more of us simply sat quietly, listened, withheld our assumptions. If we swapped certainty for curiosity, if we remembered how much we can learn by being wrong. The philosopher Simone Weil said, “Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity.” What a gift, then, to be present with another's thoughts, their voice, their story, even for a few minutes.

Today, as you read this—wherever you are—I hope you find a moment of pause amid all the noise. Perhaps you’ll notice the trees bending in the wind outside your window, or hear the laughter of someone you love. Maybe you’ll recall a favorite song, or the taste of something you once shared long ago. These small things stitch together into the tapestry of a life.

If the task is to write whatever comes to mind, maybe what comes to me most is gratitude. For the shape of quiet mornings, for books well-loved and battered with use, for the borrowed wisdom of friends and teachers, and for the chance to reach—even in this strange and digital way—toward another consciousness looking back.

We live in a world hungry for certainty, yet so often what sustains us is mystery: the blank space at the edge of a map, the hush before dawn, the not-knowing. I think of the countless stories left untold, the lives intersecting with mine in ways I’ll never realize. It’s humbling and beautiful to imagine all the simultaneous truths that can coexist—the pain and the hope, the loss and the possibility.

I don’t know what tomorrow will bring, or what will happen after you finish reading. Perhaps you’ll forget these words, or perhaps a fragment will linger the next time you find yourself alone in the quiet. That, too, is enough. The poet Mary Oliver wrote, “Instructions for living a life: / Pay attention. / Be astonished. / Tell about it.” I think that’s the best any of us can hope for—to see, to marvel, to pass it on.

So, here at the end of these one thousand words, I thank you for your company. I hope that somewhere in the vast, interconnected web of things, you’ll feel a flicker of recognition—a reminder that, in the act of reading and writing, we are never truly alone. The world is both enormous and intimate; our lives are small yet infinite in meaning, knit together by the stories we share and the attention we give.

Go well.
